fields.name: 4. Is social media addiction a growing concern?
fields.content-01:
1. Warm-up & Introduction (10 minutes)

‍

  • Briefly introduce the topic: social media addiction and why it matters today
  • Explain the World Schools (WS) debate format in a simplified way (roles, timing, affirmative vs. negative)
  • Present the core motion for the debate:
    This House believes that social media addiction is a growing societal problem.

‍

2. Guided Discussion: Social Media Use (10 minutes)

‍

Whole-class discussion led by the teacher. Suggested questions:

  • Which social media platforms do you use most often?
  • How much time do you think you spend on social media per day?
  • What do you mostly use social media for (communication, entertainment, information, validation)?
  • What are the positive effects of social media?
  • When does usage become a problem?

(Teacher briefly maps key points on the board.)

‍

3. Brainstorming Motions & Case Ideas (15 minutes)

‍

Step 1: Brainstorming (5 minutes)

  • In small groups, students brainstorm arguments for and against the idea that social media addiction is a serious problem.
  • Prompt them to think about:
    • Mental health
    • Education and productivity
    • Social relationships
    • Personal responsibility vs. platform responsibility

Step 2: Case Building (10 minutes)

  • Divide students into Affirmative and Negative teams
  • Each team builds a simple case:
    • Definition of social media addiction
    • 2 main arguments
    • Examples or evidence
    • Expected arguments from the other side

‍

4. Research & Preparation (15 minutes)

‍

  • Students may use phones/tablets to find:
    • Statistics
    • Expert opinions
    • Real-life examples
  • Teams finalize their case and assign speaker roles:
    • 1st speaker: definition and main argument
    • 2nd speaker: additional argument and rebuttal

‍

5. Mini World Schools Debate (25 minutes)

‍

Debate Structure:

  • 1st Affirmative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • 1st Negative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • 2nd Affirmative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • 2nd Negative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • 3rd Affirmative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • 3rd Negative Speaker – 3 minutes
  • Short Open Floor / Audience Questions – 7 minutes

(Teacher moderates time and ensures respectful debate.)

‍

6. Debrief & Reflection (10 minutes)

‍

  • Class discussion:
    • Which arguments were most persuasive and why?
    • What was difficult about defending your side?
    • Did any argument change your opinion?
  • Short written reflection (optional):
    • One thing you learned
    • One skill you practiced

‍

7. Closing (5 minutes)

‍

  • Powerful quote for reflection:
    "Social media is the crack cocaine of the digital world." – Simon Mainwaring
  • Thought-provoking question:
    "Are you using your device, or is your device using you?" – Denzel Washington

Encourage students to observe their own social media habits over the next few days.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • Develop critical thinking about social media use
  • Enhance debate, argumentation, and case-building skills
  • Improve research and information synthesis abilities
  • Practice public speaking and active listening
  • Expand vocabulary related to social media and addiction
fields.content-03:
Learning Outcomes

‍

‍By the end of the lesson, students will be able to:

  • Articulate clear arguments for and against a motion related to social media addiction
  • Analyze the social, psychological, and educational impacts of social media use
  • Respond to opposing viewpoints through rebuttal
  • Demonstrate basic understanding of the World Schools debate format
  • Reflect on their own social media habits in a critical way
fields.content-04:
Materials and Tools

‍

  • Tablets or mobile phones with internet access
  • Whiteboard and markers
  • Timer
  • Debate structure handouts (WS format)
  • Debate scoring sheets (optional)
  • Short evaluation/reflection sheets
fields.content-05:
Evaluation (Optional)

‍

  • Participation in discussion and debate
  • Quality of arguments and rebuttals
  • Respectful listening and teamwork
  • Reflection sheet responses

‍

Tips and tricks

‍

‍Encourage students to use specific examples and statistics to support their arguments and to make us of the given handouts
